item.dc:creator: deafkitten

item.title: Every morning and night I send my adult kids dad jokes, daily theme songs, funny memes, heartwarming shit, and the occasional nightmare fuel as a goodnight kiss. Here’s the latest.
item.description:
Good morning, cherubs. Time to get this bread.
I may not get a dog, but I could be talked into a capybara.
?️‍? ?️‍⚧️
Good morning, houseplants.
Here come the dad jokes. True story: when my kids come home from their house or from work I often — to this day — meet them in the front yard by lifting my shirt and slapping my belly. Pavlov’s reaction has kicked in and now they do it, too, even in front of their partner / prospective partners. This is a message that means, “Prepare for the Silly.”
Anyone? Is this thing on?
Bmm tsa bmm tssa brrrrt
My kids ask me why I am the way that I am. I showed them this clip and told them this is what cartoons were like when I was younger.
This is beautiful. Make sure you turn on your sound. Turn it up or you might miss it as it is an old recording.
Did you see it the first time?
This is why they don’t let me dance at weddings anymore.
Ok, this is serious talent. Look who is in the booth!
Anyone know where this is from?
Time to wake up, I guess. My mornings are mostly like this.
Good night. Sweet dreams.
Me trying to get up in the morning.
This is the other reason I’m not allowed to dance at weddings. I meet fellow dancers.
Good night. I love you. I’m proud of you. I’m grateful you’re in my life.
1. She can really sing. 2. She is totally right about the shoes. You can tell by how he reacts. 3. I have little daddy house shoes too. 4. I don’t know about you, but I want to find a wife who sings with such wild abandon and also puts her legs up in the air like that when she’s having fun.

item.title: Tanya Roberts as Sheena (1984)
item.description:
Roberts (1949 - 2021) is known from Charlies Angels, the Bond film A View to a Kill, the sword & sorcery classic The Beastmaster, and as Midge on That '70s Show
Sheena, Queen of the Jungle, originated as a 1938 US comic book hero in the vein of Tarzan, and popularized the jungle girl-heroines common in the '40s & '50s
She is an orphan raised by Africans into a skilled outdoorsman, martial fighter, and by a shaman stepparent to communicate with animals [telepathically in the film]
She is important as the first female character with her own paper (1941) yet the dated legacy Sheena sprung from does the film no favours - unfortunately it's not good regardless
As described by critic Leonard Maltin the cinematography and music are good, and Roberts swings as the "comic-book jungle-queen, but Mother Nature forgot to endow her with a script. Supposed to be campy, but it doesn't work even on that level;"
To its advantage, being filmed in Kenya, it includes African actors - for ex is the stepmother portrayed by Elizabeth of Tooro, an Ugandan princess, lawyer and model - as well as fair landscapes
Still, not all wildlife was local. Entertaining trivia is that the zebra of Sheena was a horse with painted stripes: a rare case of the horse in more make-up than the actress
As for the dashing Roberts the producers knew their casting, since it has attracted viewers ever since; and some uncropped screen shots would get me banned from imgur...
To quote critic Roger Ebert: "It's probably the only PG-rated movie that will play continuously on the Playboy Channel—you see more of Tanya Roberts than you did of last month's playmate."
Sheena failed at the box office, still Roberts said of it: "It was the hardest film I've ever worked on but at the same time the most gratifying. It was so amazingly beautiful in Africa, the wildlife, the sunrises and sunsets, the skies, the land, and the people."
item.dc:creator: Ngugi

item.title: Dumb preppers think gold and silver will save them for the kind of societal collapse they are preparing for
item.description: My grand parents told me, one solid gold bracelet worth half a chicken, one gold ring can get you a sausage or a few loaves of bread. Actual valuable things are medicine, food, even candles, everyday essentials. The ones that never go hungry are blacksmith, carpenters, notice I didn't mention electrician or plumber because there won't be any electricity or running water; doctors will still live a good life, lawyers will all die, accountants will go hungry. The ones that will never worry about long term living are farmers. Best way to prep for social collapse is to not allowing it to happen in the first place.
item.dc:creator: Cliperinoposterino

item.title: Anyone who can speak Charlie Kirk's name without condemning him for what he is enables fascist assaults on freedom like this, especially if they are a politician, public figure, or work in media.
item.description: There should be absolutely no equivocation about condemning this man and celebrating his death. He was a violent white supremacist who spent his entire life advocating for white supremacy, advocating the murder of all queer people, and advocating fascism. He was a nazi shitstain and if you're not glad he's dead, if you feel bad for him, if you begin any criticism of him with "of course he didn't deserve this" then you are a fascist sympathizer. He did deserve this. Our country is run by fascists like this. Their ongoing existence is a threat to innocent people, a threat to democracy, and a threat to the freedom of all Americans.
item.dc:creator: Freyja33

item.title: Charlie Kirk is a distraction - DO NOT TAKE YOUR EYES OFF GAZA
item.dc:creator: chukyrlaw

item.title: MAGA Man Threatens To Shoot Democrats In Florida Store While Staring At The One Black Female Employee
item.description: MAGA Man: "I'm ready," he yells repeatedly. "I'm locked and loaded for Democrats." Female Employee: "I don't know why he's looking at me when he said that," she says to coworker. The woman, who goes by "Canna-Montana," said she was afraid to point the camera directly at the man during the confrontation. "So he can come across this video online and then come back to the store? Damn, you want me to get shot?" she replied. "In my lil town, the cops would've come and said, 'What did he look like?' I would've said, 'Bald, white, and sunglasses.' What would've realistically happened next? I don't waste my time when I know the outcome." The incident occurred in Leesburg, Florida.

item.dc:creator: countervail247365

item.title: When your stupidity leads to a federal law to protect the stupidity of others
item.description:
REPOST: I had to fix an error in legal terminology. . One source of contention about plane crashes in the news is the government’s refusal to release the cockpit recordings of the plane crash. It’s seen as either very suspicious, or just frustrating because it was a horrific public event that should include ALL of the aspects of it (if that makes any sense). The reason that the US government won’t release the cockpit recordings of plane crashes is because they legally can’t.
The official law name is very memorable: Pub. L. 97–309, §2, 96 Stat. 1453
I’m not a pilot, so I can’t even begin to explain what mechanically caused the plane to crash, because I don’t understand it myself. The official report said that the pilot took off without the wing flaps and slats in the correct position because he was too busy fucking around with the other crew members. Their official words: “We determined that the probably cause of this accident to be: “(1) the Captain and First Officer's inadequate cockpit discipline which resulted in the flightcrew's attempt to takeoff without the wing flaps and slats properly configured; and “(2) the failure of the takeoff configuration warning system to alert the crew that the airplane was not properly configured for the takeoff.” Fortunately, I found an animation, followed by the actual CVR recording.
I wish there had been a closeup of the flaps. I'd like to know what position they were supposed to be at.
“Then the media will have something juicy to listen to.” Well, they sure did! I guess it was *too* juicy for their comfort…
Like the meme says, this experience was so embarrassing that pilots were outraged, and lobbied for CVRs to remain confidential at all times. The government reinstated and kept Pub. L. 97–309, §2, 96 Stat. 1453; in 1990, and it’s been in effect ever since, though new things have been added over the years. . Note: CVRs remaining confidential is more about keeping the audio recordings confidential in cases of plane crashes. Nobody is looking to scrutinize a CVR from a standard flight where nothing happened, unless they suspect the flight crew of inappropriate behavior, and even then that’s not something that the public needs to know about. This law is to protect pilots from embarrassment when flying a plane that crashed because they were not doing what they were supposed to be doing.
The law itself was originally made in the 1970s, but was repealed. After the tomfoolery of Flight 1141, it was reinstated.
A completely different plane crash that was not brought on by arrogance and stupidity.
Delta Airlines Flight 191 was a horrific plane caused by a highly catastrophic weather event called a “microburst.” A microburst to my understanding is when stormy weather pushes a sudden blast of cold air downward. Microbursts are very small, and barely detectable. At the time of Flight 191, microbursts weren’t well known. There definitely weren’t any emergency detectors for it at the time.
You can see the struggle the pilots had with trying to get the plane under control, and how catastrophic a microburst can be. The one good thing that came out of this horrific tragedy is that the plane industry took this tragedy to heart, and immediately worked on trying to both understand microbursts better, and build a microburst warning system for unsuspecting pilots.
In no part of the cockpit recording did the pilots discuss the dating lives of the flight attendants. That flight crew fought HARD to safely land.
It might bring you a little bit of joy to know that the pilots of Flight 1141 were fired, and blacklisted from the industry. Unfortunately, the mechanic was also blacklisted despite investigators determining that he wasn’t at fault. However, he was able to get his job back after an appeal. But yes, this dumb fuck crew right here is why we can’t have nice things. . . . EDIT/ADDENDUM:. I know that I possibly jarred a few folks that have listened to CVR recordings with my sarcastic attitude. I don’t think that death by plane crash is a joke. I don’t think it needs to be a museum where we see gory pictures of the bodies or anything like that. However, a CVR recording is a testimony to either how terrible a pilot is, or how valiant a pilot is. I was profoundly moved by the CVR of Delta Flight 191 because those pilots FOUGHT the sky to keep the plane in a stable position. They lost that fight, but what they did brought us to the awareness of microbursts, and how lethal they can be. Nobody reasonable wants to hear someone die. It’s not a form of entertainment. I’m sorry if my sardonic tone made it seem like I wasn’t taking it seriously. The reason this law came into place is absolutely absurd. It wasn’t the sound of Flight 191 pilots saying “Oh shit!” As they crashed to their deaths. It was the CVR of the pilots mocking the CVR of Flight 191. The CVR of Delta Flight 1141 is the reason that CVS recordings should be available to the general public. The one where they mocked the crew of Flight 191, talked about the dating lives of the flight attendants, and the crash where the fourteen people that were killed were passengers. This confidentiality law wasn’t about respect for the dead. It was about being embarrassed that they were caught screwing around. I was mocking the fact that this was the tragedy that sparked the outrage. I apologize if I made anyone that has heard CVRs feel that I was mocking their trauma, or anyone’s death. Death is a very serious and heavy thing, and should be treated with respect. Our definition of respect might be different, but I’m not saying: LOLOL HASHTAG PLANE CRASH!!
Investigation: https://www.ntsb.gov/investigations/Pages/DCA88MA072.aspx Cockpit Recording: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XARLjDjWkYk The Federal Law: https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/49/1114 The Federal Law 2: https://www.govinfo.gov/content/pkg/USCODE-2023-title49/pdf/USCODE-2023-title49-subtitleII-chap11-subchapII-sec1114.pdf Pub. L. 97–309, §2, 96 Stat. 1453: https://www.congress.gov/97/statute/STATUTE-96/STATUTE-96-Pg1453.pdf Flight 1141 Animation (someone posted this but I checked the original source): https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jq1tieochaw Flight 191 Animation: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=M4V5NqcDDVA Flight 191 and the Microburst: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=HDfodeURad0 Delta 191 Overview: https://tailstrike.com/database/2-august-1985-delta-191/ Delta 191 CVR Transcript: https://mail.aviation-safety.net/investigation/transcripts/cvr_dl191.php Microbursts: https://pilotinstitute.com/microbursts/

item.title: 100-Year-Old Candy Store Register Re-Restoration: Electric Boogaloo
item.description:
Oh, naive Ellie. You thought it'd just be a quick little weekend project. Look at her. Point at her and laugh. I've been quietly grumbling about this cash register since last year, ever since two of the price flags collided and the whole thing jammed. I was a little more torqued off because I'd been planning to gift it to my mom and dad. So, a month and a half ago I finally shook off my funk and pulled it down off its shelf. Once it hits the workbench, I have to start working on it. Them's the rules. For context, this is a cash register that I restored in 2023, right when I was first feeling my way around the hobby. At the time, I had a few screwdrivers to my name, a shiny new buffing wheel, and a saved URL to a now defunct website that an oldbeard was selling spare cash register parts out of. This was my third or maybe fourth register I'd ever worked on, and back then I was utterly confident that if it was working when I bought it, it would stay working forever. Oh, Ellie. You simple, simple woman.
Still, I knew it was going to test me. The milk glass slab was chipped and jagged, the rosettes holding it in place were from a different machine, all the keys were worn through and needed replacements, the end caps were gone and replaced by a wood block, and the price flags had seen better days. And that's just what I knew about when I first restored it.
And the end result turned out beautifully. Shiny as hell, clean, and working like a charm. That was three years ago, and boy howdy did the chickens come home to roost. (cue ominous music)
Wakkabam! Fast forward to late 2025 and it's fully seized up and that shiny clean patina is turning all kinds of strange. Some apologies are in order. When I started taking things apart to assess the cause of the jam, I didn't plan on sharing the repair in a post because I assumed it would be a quick in and out. I didn't get a photo of what it looked like before I broke it down to the mechanism, but the color had darkened (which is normal) to a deeper gold patina, and large swaths of the stippling between the scrollwork had turned almost black (very not normal) where a less experienced me hadn't removed all of the polishing compound. We'll get to more about that later. Until then, let's talk about the mechanism:
So. Again, I was kind of negligent on the before shots until I realized this would be a larger project than I planned for. That epiphany came after I lugged the whole thing out to the garage to utterly flood the thing with penetrating oil. Why? Well, take a peek at the next clip: ----v
The camera is moving so much because of the sheer amount of force I had to put into moving the 5¢ lever. That's beyond abnormal behavior. That's weewoo-weewoo critical anomaly shit. It also didn't feel great to pull the prices flags out, expecting them to be the sole cause of the jam since they'd collided, only to learn there was something mechanically wrong going on besides that. It took me several days to finally work out where the problem was. My first thought was that it had to do with the middlemost counter that engages whenever something is rung up. My attention was stuck on the pawl arm that pushes the 5¢ wheel forward because. I was convinced that something was going on inside the mechanism to cause it to stick, or not cycle completely, because it's the most immediately visible moving part. It was also a red herring.
Still, I needed to bang my head against this complicated mess before I was going to learn. When I first restored it, I never did anything with the innards because everything was already working. I dusted it a little and closed it up. Don't fix what's not broken, right? Well, sometimes right. Sometimes wrong. Before I started taking pictures of this revisit, all of these parts were caked in old grime and a lot of dust. It took a lot of penetrating oil and wiping just to get it looking this clean, which is saying something.
And ultimately, it was a lot of good preventative maintenance that didn't actually solve the jamming problem at all. This counter? It's working fine, and it wasn't the root cause. Just a very visible symptom of a problem that was occurring a few steps earlier in the chain of movements. Rather than it being an issue with the counter, the problem was with the 5¢ lever itself and, to some extent, its design. Again, I don't have great photos because its buried so deep in the register and also because I didn't think to try to get a photo before I put it all back together. So, some explaining time again! If you look at the earlier photos, you'll see that the keys you press are welded to levers that are flat, stamped parts turned onto their narrow side. Deeper in the register is a pivot rod that slots through a hole in all of the levers. It's a simple design and one that works very well... right up until the end of that lever is allowed to slide a little to the left or right. From the pivot rod's perspective, when the lever stops being anything but perfectly perpendicular to it, the hole it's meant to pass through becomes an oval and the two parts end up binding. That's what happened in this case. The 5¢ lever slid out of alignment when I took all the panels off for this repair project.
So, after several days of puzzling over what had broken down, I put the spacing plate back on and the whole problem vanished. Did I feel stupid for not trying this earlier? Yup! Am I going to remember to test this out the next time it happens? You bet your technicolor ass I will. (Also, the bell isn't broken, I took it out because it was caving in my eardrums while I was troubleshooting this mess.)
Now that the mechanical problems had been resolved, I moved on to dealing with the price flags. While this step was more optional than necessary, I wanted an excuse to test out my new Cricut machine and see how the decals I made compared to the ones I was buying for $50 a sheet. The "No Sale" flag on this register was fine, but the font that came with the set I'd bought on eBay were an extremely plain, narrow font that makes Arial look like a party. And while this was the font that NCR did choose to use, I cannot stand how much it stands out like a sore thumb compared to the ornate font they used for their numerals and scrollwork. So I said to heck with it, I'm going to replace it with something I think fits a little better.
I had to take some measurements to make sure the decals would fit the flag, and in this case first time was actually the charm. Some transfer paper applied to the top of the decals is enough to pull them off their adhesive paper and allow me to apply them to the blank flag. This is exactly the same process used by the guy who was charging me fifty bucks a set, and I daresay mine look a helluva lot crisper.
Pretty easy! The vinyl adheres smoothly without any trouble, and stays put. I left it unlacquered in case I decide to take it back out and replace it with the original, unadorned font NCR used. Until then, I think I like this version quite a bit better.
After identifying the flag that caused the original jam in the first place, I found that I had mistakenly overlapped them in the wrong order which caused one to run up into the other. I spent some time staring down into this bay to make sure I hadn't repeated that mistake somewhere else, and thankfully I had the foresight and knowledge this time to manually lift each flag by hand to verify things would be okay to close up.
It also helps to know that the bends in the stems are always meant to alternate in and out. Something that's still on my list of parts to find are replacement springs for the flag stems. They're very often broken, like this one, and their purpose is to compress when a flag is raised so that they can shove the flag back down once the register resets. Though most of these springs are broken or worn down, they do still accomplish that purpose once the flag is raised. It's not something this register or the others I've worked on need, but it bugs me and I'd like to start fixing these as I work on future projects.
Now, the cash drawer. When I restored this back in 2023 I barely had the right screwdrivers to use, say nothing for any woodworking tools or supplies. In my mind, "restoring" these dirty cash drawers involved ripping them apart and gluing them back together. Not something I was prepared materially or mentally to tackle for fear of screwing something up that I couldn't undo. So I washed out the drawer as best I could, gave it some wood oil, and closed it up.
Since then, I've torn down one such cash drawer and successfully rebuilt it. And while that's one data point and not a trend I'm willing to stake my reputation on, it's still something that I accomplished and can put experience toward on this project. In the case of this little guy, everything is glued in so well that I was afraid I'd end up cracking the dividers before I ever got them free. So rather than make a small project into a massive one, I opted to do what I could while keeping it intact.
Fast forward to a LOT of sanding and a LOT of cracked fingertips. This would have probably been a lot easier with some fancy electric sanding gadget, but all I got are my hands, so I spent a good hour tearing off squares and manually scraping out as much of the old lacquer, metal stains, and other grossness as I could. Getting into the corners was no bueno, but I got it done.
A couple coats of stain and some tung oil later, and it's looking better than it used to. It's far from perfect and that's alright by me. I just wanted it to look better than it did. Also, bonus points for finding a scrap piece of trim on a spare drawer and cutting it down to length to replace the one that's missing at the back of this one. Now to let that finish drying and move onto the next part of the re-restore.
So... this dust cover. Best thing I can say about it is that it's intact. It's nickel plated iron, which seems like a strange choice for a brass register, and my feeling is that it's not the original. The sheet metal design was used in the later models, when fake wood paneling paint jobs were more in vogue. This register should have one that's cast from bronze and has its own scrollwork design.
Here especially, in the old photos I have of it when I first restored it, you can see how it doesn't match the style of the register. It's also why I never opened the lid after it was finished.
Since it's clearly not original, or even remotely matching the style of the register, I'm not willing to pour the time and effort into fixing the nickel plating. Finding a replacement that has the same windows in the same locations, and doesn't come with a cash register already attached, is also not a reasonable option.
So, since I'm stuck with what I have, and what I have isn't worth bending over backwards to make shiny and perfect, I'm going to commit the cardinal sin of PAINT.
Two shots of satin black rattle can later, it looks better than it used to and should actually compliment the brass rather than clash. We'll have to see.
Speaking of brass, did you know there are different kinds? Neither did I when I first worked on this register! So imagine my surprise as the patinas darkened when I noticed the endcaps at the very top were looking mighty yellow compared to the reddening brass they were attached to. This is why they're called yellow and red brass. Oh joy, oh joy, I have the wrong freaking endcaps and never noticed. Luckily, after restoring several of these monsters since then, I have a bunch of spare parts lying around.
And by some MIRACLE, I lucked out and happened to have a set of endcaps in the correct metal and size. Only the design beneath the ledge is wrong, this one being called "Empire" by NCR while the correct design is "Victorian." Oh freaking well, sometimes you gotta eat some raisins with your trail mix. We're pissing with the cock we've got.
Which brings us to the exterior panels. Normally, I don't bring panels downstairs when they're looking as shiny as these ones are. Oh, also, ignore that dust cover being silver again. I'm kind of posting some of these out of order for the sake of a clean narrative. In reality I was doing all of this shit at the same time. <_<
The reason I'm dragging these to the utility sink is easier to see here. When I first polished these up and washed off the buffing compound, I didn't get all of it. I let myself be bamboozled by the shiny brass enough that I either didn't notice or flat out ignored how much compound was still on there. What I do remember is that I was testing out the new rotary tool I'd bought to help clean up the scrollwork, and I don't think I realized that using it to clean out the stippling was a bad idea.
I wasn't removing the compound so much as heating it up to a liquid again and smearing it around. You can see how a lot of those dimples are filled in, and over time it's started to catch dust and hang onto it to make it darker. Also, along the edges, you can see the beginning of brass oxidation where the compound is trapping moisture. All of that needs to come out.
Here's another shot of missed compound, and fresh oxidation. Running the panels through the buffing wheel gets everything shiny, sure, but once that compound cools down again it's a literal pain to scrub out. What's worse is that I didn't know at the time that the brass had been lacquered, and where that lacquer was still hanging on preserved the brass's original brightness while the surrounding metal darkened as it developed a patina. I knew before I could do anything else, I had to scrape off the rest of the old lacquer. That meant at least an hour at the sink with scotch pads, followed by another hour and change degreasing away the old buffing compound in water just hot enough that I could stand it. That would get me down to clean metal (as reasonably as I could achieve by hand). The decision I had to make was whether I wanted to then take that clean metal and run it through the buffing wheel, caking it in new lacquer, and then spend another hour or so bent over the utility sink scrubbing it away while hoping I wasn't scratching the new polish. In my mind, that sounded a lot like a cat chasing its own tail.
So I made the decision to clean the metal, remove the lacquer, and skip the polishing step. This meant the high spots in the metal were going to be bright, but they were also going to be satin rather than glossy. I've only done that with one brass register so far, and I remember very much enjoying how it looked in the end. I spent more than a couple hours using toothpicks and brushes to scour off as much of the old compound as I could get at. Thankfully, I didn't have to get very aggressive with the scotchbrite to remove the old bits of lacquer. Suffice to say, I was encouraged to see how good it looked. :)
But instead of leaving things there, I decided to take a risk and go a step further. Instead of going straight to reassembly, I took the cleaned panels out to the garage and gave every one of them two coats of glossy lacquer enamel to protect the new shine and keep it from reoxidizing.
Doing so brought the brass up to a surface texture somewhere between satin and gloss, and I was able to somehow do it without overapplying or getting any droplets onto the coating.
My hope is that I've done it well enough that it'll keep its rose gold coloration for a few happy decades, or at least until the next person gets a hold of it and decides they want to try their hand at restoration. :)
This is my first time putting lacquer on, and I think it's going to become a regular practice going forward. I'm still walking on air with how well this turned out. ^_^
And with that part done, all that's left is to put it all back together! So, as always, we have to start with the bottom and build our way up.
In goes the mechanism, and a dozen chunky screws secure it in place through the bottom.
Next, as always, is the back plate. I really need to find another candy store register. These things weigh barely anything compared to their full sized counterparts.
Once the back plate is secure, the sides go on. These get screwed in through the bottom and the sides, but aren't fully tightened just yet.
Same process on the opposite side.
Only after the crosspiece is fitted between them do they get tightened down. I love the crosspiece for this register because it gets screwed in from the outside, rather than forcing me to dangle four screws underneath it on the inside.
Next comes the lever guide, the tines of each fit into slots cast into the iron mechanism.
And while I'm down there, I might as well put the marble shelf down.
Of course, I needed extra long screws to tighten down the guard that hides the bottom of the lever guide, which meant spending forty bucks on McMaster-Carr since I'd run out. There's always a catch. ;)
Next is the lid, including the lock that gets screwed in from the back.
Loving how clean the mechanisms are looking, now that they've been drowned in penetrating oil and lubricant. Smells like smooth operation, baby.
And of course, it's time to see if painting that dust cover worked as well as I hoped.
Yowza. For a part that's about 30 years younger than the register it found itself on, it turned out to be a hell of a looker. The paint got its own hit of lacquer to protect the finish, so it should be able to take some abuse before that crummy old nickel rears its ugly head.
And while I didn't plan this, I noticed once it was installed that the silver and black of the dust cover and its hardware perfectly match the silver and black of the key labels in front of it! Heck yeah, bonus!
Moving around the side, a couple hinges hang the printer door.
*aggressive chefkissing sounds* Augh it looks so good!
In goes the glass, after a quick hit of Windex.
And now that the drawer is dry, we can get him upstairs and back where he belongs.
And voila! The completed re-restoration of a 1925 National Cash Register! It's looking pretty dang good, and just in time for its 100th birthday!
Finally, it's ready to escape the clutches of the basement, garage, and workshop to roam the wilds of the coveted -living room- ooooh! Seriously though, I love the satin finish on all the high spots more than the mirror finish I initially gave it. The softer tones make my brain tingle in all the right ways, and knowing that it's mechanically sound and won't lose its brightness for a long time makes that feel even better.
And for the first time, I'm actually glad to lift the lid on this little thing. <3
I still can't get over how much difference a coat of paint made.
And to give you all an idea of its size compared to the monstrously large crank register I finished before it, this thing's barely a third the width of its deformed Frankenbro. Looks a heck of a lot better, too. ;)
And no restoration post would be complete without the obligatory proof-of-ding! Woohoo! And good god, I started making this post at seven and it's creeping past nine-thirty at night. I need to wrap this up and get to bed so I wake up in time to fire it into usersub tomorrow!
Thanks for coming on another long and winding restoration journey with me, everyone! I know this one was a little different than my normal posts, and I hope it helped some of you to see some of the mistakes I made the first time around. The main takeaway I wanted to share with this post is that when you start learning something new, it's okay to make and miss newbie mistakes. I made my share, and three years later I was able to come back around and do a better job. And hey, I'm still learning! With every restoration I attempt, I'm finding new problems to fix and new gaps in my knowledge to patch up. And a large part of why I'm able to do any of this is because of the support you all continue to share with me. Whether it's the constant enthusiasm that keeps me motivated or the financial support that shores up the costs of each restoration, having a place where I can share this part of my life has been huge. So thank you to everyone who has sent a kind word my way or dropped a few coins into the restoration fund. I'd still be working off my coffee table with a pair of old screwdrivers if it weren't for you. For anyone who would like to chip in towards the next cash register, here's where you can do it: https://ko-fi.com/elbowdeepinahorse
item.dc:creator: ElbowDeepInAHorse
